ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Paneer Tikka Masala exists; mapped to the broader 'curry dish' class (FOODON_03302051) as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for paneer-based curry dishes is not currently present in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Paneer (Indian cottage cheese) — used as the primary ingredient anchor; no composite Paneer Tikka Masala entry exists in FoodEx2. Mapped to paneer ingredient node as the closest defensible code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a composite entry for Paneer Tikka Masala. Code A0CKP (paneer) is used as the primary ingredient anchor. The composite curry preparation is not individually coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Paneer Tikka Masala or equivalent paneer curry dish. SNOMED CT does not routinely code prepared Indian curry dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.description: 1 restaurant portion (approximately 300 g, served with rice or bread)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Paneer Tikka Masala in Singapore is approximately 300 g, usually served alongside steamed basmati rice or naan bread as part of a set meal.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer Tikka Masala — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central composite estimation for paneer-based curry
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry for Paneer Tikka Masala as a composite restaurant dish exists. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 data for paneer (cottage cheese), tomato-based curry gravy, and cream components, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for paneer (FDC ID 2003590) and Indian curry sauce. The per-100g values represent a weighted composite of approximately 40% paneer, 50% tomato-cream masala sauce, and 10% oil/ghee, consistent with standard restaurant-style preparation. Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of Indian curry dishes and paneer-based preparations — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tomato-based curry and paneer
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Paneer Tikka Masala exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous entries: (1) tomato-based curry dishes with legumes/dairy GI ~40–50 from the Sydney GI Database; (2) paneer (Indian cottage cheese) has a very low GI (~15–20) due to high protein and fat content; (3) the masala sauce contains tomatoes, cream, and spices with negligible glycemic impact. The composite GI of approximately 45 is defensible given the dominant paneer and cream components. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (45 × 7.4 × 3) / 100 = 9.99, rounded to 9.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per 300 g restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single restaurant serving of Paneer Tikka Masala provides approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing nearly 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg for adults with hypertension; individuals managing blood pressure should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) driven by high protein and fat content from paneer and cream, with moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Tikka Masala has a low glycemic index of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of approximately 9.9 per 300 g serving, making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the high saturated fat content from cream and paneer warrants attention in the context of overall dietary fat management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~5.4 g per 100 g; ~16.2 g per 300 g serving) from paneer, cream, and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Paneer Tikka Masala contributes approximately 16 g of saturated fat, which exceeds the recommended daily limit of 14–20 g for adults with cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with CVD should limit portion size, request preparation with reduced cream and ghee, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Tikka Masala is a popular North Indian vegetarian curry dish consisting of cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) that are first marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture and then grilled in a tandoor (clay oven) to achieve a characteristic smoky, charred exterior. The grilled paneer is subsequently simmered in a rich, thick tomato-based masala sauce made with onions, garlic, ginger, cream, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, turmeric, and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves). The dish has a creamy, mildly tangy flavour profile with a deep orange-red colour derived from tomatoes and spices. In Singapore, it is widely served in North Indian restaurants and is a staple on Indian restaurant menus, typically accompanied by basmati rice, naan, or roti. Nutritionally, Paneer Tikka Masala is a moderate-energy dish (~148 kcal per 100 g) with a notable protein contribution (~7.8 g per 100 g) from paneer, significant saturated fat (~5.4 g per 100 g) from cream and paneer, and a low glycemic index (~45) making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is high in calcium due to the paneer content. Clinically, the sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g) is a concern for individuals managing hypertension, and the saturated fat load is relevant for cardiovascular disease risk management. It is naturally gluten-free and suitable for lacto-vegetarian and halal diets.

confidence_reason: No exact Paneer Tikka Masala composite entry exists in ICMR-NIN 2017 or USDA FoodData Central as a single restaurant-style dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer data and USDA curry sauce components using a defensible weighted composite model. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable tomato-cream curry and paneer entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 (paneer component) and USDA FoodData Central (tomato-cream curry sauce component) using a weighted composite of approximately 40% paneer, 50% masala sauce, and 10% oil/ghee. No single approved-source entry for restaurant-style Paneer Tikka Masala exists. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA source data for this composite preparation. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source data for paneer or tomato-based curry preparations. 4. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Paneer Tikka Masala was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 5. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'Paneer Tikka Masala (Spicy)(Chef Recommendation)' to the canonical English name 'Paneer Tikka Masala'; the spicy and chef recommendation qualifiers are preserved in aliases. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the vegetarian and dairy-only composition of the dish; individual restaurant preparation may vary. 7. Sodium content will vary significantly between restaurants; the value of 380 mg per 100 g represents a mid-range estimate for restaurant-style preparation.
